summary: Gruber enthusiastically recommends Front and Center, a new Mac utility co-written by John Siracusa and Lee Fyock that restores classic Mac OS window layering behavior where clicking a background window brings all of that app's windows to the front. The app offers Classic and Modern modes with Shift-click as a modifier to toggle behavior. Gruber highlights the meticulous design process behind even the app's minimal preferences window, noting dozens of revisions in a small feedback group. He argues macOS should have offered this functionality natively for 18 years, and praises the app as carrying forward the spirit of classic Mac OS simple utilities.
key_points: Classic Mac OS brought all of an app's windows forward when clicking any one; Mac OS X changed this to only bring the clicked window forward, which Gruber and Siracusa consider a regression
The preferences window, despite appearing trivially simple, went through dozens of revisions — illustrating how much work goes into getting even small details right
The app's use of Shift-click as modifier was the only viable choice since Control, Option, Command, and Command-Option clicks all have existing system-wide functions
Front and Center embodies the spirit of classic Mac OS control panels and extensions that improved system behavior in small, focused ways
money_quotes: Honestly, the Mac OS X system should have offered all of Front and Center's functionality for the last 18 years.
It looks like five minutes of work, right? There's one pop-up menu, two checkboxes, and a bit of explanatory text explaining what the options do.
The number of revisions even this super-simple prefs window went through shows just how much work goes into getting anything just right.
But now that I have it back, I realize I've missed it.
It feels right to me that when you switch to an app by clicking one of its visible background windows, the whole app comes forward.
Classic Mac OS was chockablock with little extensions and control panels that improved the standard system behavior in small ways.
I love that Front and Center not only brings back behavior from the classic era, it does so in the spirit of classic-era simple utilities.
My pal Brent Simmons is running Front and Center just because he likes having the icon in his Dock.
key_insight: Even the simplest-looking software requires enormous craft to get right, and the best utilities are those that do one thing well in service of how users actually want to work.
